{"step_by_step_thinking": "Aortoiliac occlusive disease (AIOD) affects the aorta and iliac arteries, which are major inflow vessels to the lower extremities. According to the documents, symptoms of AIOD are typically localized to the thigh and gluteal region due to the anatomical location of the obstruction. Document [3] states that 'aortoiliac disease is referred to as inflow disease and typically produces symptoms in the thigh or gluteal musculature.' Document [10] reinforces this by noting that 'patients with aortoiliac artery occlusive disease, therefore, have symptoms in the thigh and buttock muscles.' This directly supports gluteal claudication as the primary symptom. In contrast, calf claudication is more typical of femoropopliteal disease, which occurs distal to the iliac arteries. Impotence, while a common associated symptom (especially in Leriche syndrome), is not the most common or primary symptom. The option'symptomless' is incorrect because AIOD, when symptomatic, presents with claudication. Therefore, the most common symptom of aortoiliac occlusive disease is gluteal claudication.", "answer_choice": "B"}
